papers.title: Mixed thresholds in the Lonely Runner Conjecture
papers.summary: The Lonely Runner Conjecture states that if $k+1$ runners start at the same point on a unit-length circular track and run with distinct constant speeds, then each runner is at some time at least $1/(k+1)$-distant from every other runner. Equivalently, for every tuple of $k$ distinct positive integer speeds $s_1,\ldots,s_k$, there is a real number $t$ such that $\|s_i t\|\geq \frac{1}{k+1}$ for all

papers.title: Linnik's problem for multiplicative functions
papers.summary: We study a multiplicative function analogue of Linnik's problem on the least prime in an arithmetic progression. Let $h\colon \mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{R}\setminus\{0\}$ be a multiplicative function, and let $a \pmod q$ be a reduced residue class. We ask how far one must go before finding square-free integers $n_1,n_2\equiv a \pmod q$ with $h(n_1)<0<h(n_2)$. We show that one can always find such intege

papers.title: A proof of the $4,7$ cases of Sylvester's conjecture on cube sums
papers.summary: In this paper, we prove that every prime $p$ which is congruent to $4,7$ modulo $9$ is the sum of two rational cubes. This is $2/3$ of Sylvester's conjecture which has a history of nearly 150 years since 1879. In the proof, we use recent progress on Full BSD conjecture of rank $0$ elliptic curves in \cite{BF} to deduce that the Manin-Stevens constants of some families of elliptic curves are units.

papers.title: On the binary digits of the Erdős-Borwein constant
papers.summary: In a landmark paper on arithmetical properties of Lambert series, Erdős proved that $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2^{n} - 1}$ is irrational. This value $E$ is now referred to as the Erdős-Borwein constant. Crandall, in 2012, studied properties of the base-2 expansion of this constant, and left the following as an open problem: Does the string $11$ occur infinitely often in the base-2 expansion of

papers.title: Pairs of square-free arithmetic progressions in infinite words
papers.summary: We study a question of Harju from 2019 regarding the existence of infinite ternary square-free words whose subsequences modulo $p$ and $q$ are also square-free for relatively prime integers $p$ and $q$. Among such pairs $(p, q)$ with $p, q \geq 3$, the only two pairs with this property known prior to this work were $(3, 11)$ and $(5, 6)$. We prove that there are finitely many pairs $(p, q)$ of rel

papers.title: Finding a Solution to the Erdős-Ginzburg-Ziv Theorem in Linear Time
papers.summary: The Erdős-Ginzburg-Ziv theorem states that every sequence of 2n - 1 integers contains a subsequence of length n whose sum is divisible by n. Choi, Kang, and Lim gave a simple deterministic O(n log n) algorithm for finding such a subsequence, and Leung recently improved this to O(n log log log n). We give a deterministic linear-time algorithm. The core is a linear-time algorithm for the following p

papers.title: Box Progressions, Abelian Power-Free Morphisms and A Sieve Technique for the Template Method
papers.summary: Given balls and boxes both enumerated by the positive integers, we consider a sequential allocation of the balls into the boxes. We fix $\ell \ge 2$. Proceeding in increasing order of box labels, assign to each box the next $r$ smallest balls for some $ 1\leq r\leq\ell$. Given an integer $k\ge 3$, is there a natural number $N$ such that in any placement of $N$ balls into boxes, there exist $k$ bal

top_analogs.structural_map: Proving a sharp upper bound on the size of a set that avoids 3-term arithmetic progressions using algebraic rank.
top_analogs.obstruction: The slice rank method is specifically optimized for vector spaces over finite fields (F_q^n) rather than the infinite set of integers.

top_analogs.structural_map: Finding small structured configurations (corners) in a set, which serves as a multidimensional analog to 3-term APs.
top_analogs.obstruction: Mapping the one-dimensional sequence of powerful numbers into a higher-dimensional grid suitable for corner analysis is non-trivial.

top_analogs.structural_map: The condition of having consecutive terms in an AP is equivalent to the gap sequence containing a square (pattern 'dd'), mapping adjacency to word repetitions.
top_analogs.obstruction: Powerful numbers are defined by multiplicative properties (p | n implies p^2 | n) rather than being generated by the fixed points of a morphism.

top_analogs.structural_map: The existence of 3-term arithmetic progressions in a subset of integers of a given density or growth rate.
top_analogs.obstruction: Powerful numbers are extremely sparse (density zero), falling below the threshold where classical Roth-type density results guarantee the existence of APs.

top_analogs.named_technique: hypergraph removal lemma / arithmetic removal lemma (Green 2005; Král-Serra-Vena 2009)
top_analogs.structural_map: The equation x+z=2y for a 3-term AP is the same linear-configuration skeleton as the target triples n_k,n_{k+1},n_{k+2}.
top_analogs.obstruction: Removal lemmas are density tools for large finite ambient groups, while powerful numbers are a zero-density deterministic sequence.

technique_cards.name: Slice rank method

seminal_paper.citation: Ellenberg and Gijswijt, Ann. of Math. 185(1):339-343

technique_cards.what_it_does: The slice rank method gives strong upper bounds for progression-free subsets of finite vector spaces by bounding the rank of tensors encoding forbidden additive patterns. It is especially effective for cap-set type problems over finite fields, where algebraic dimension replaces interval length or density in the integers.
input_requirements: a finite vector space F_q^n or comparable finite-field product structure
a forbidden additive pattern expressible by a low-degree polynomial or tensor constraint
a subset whose size is to be bounded by algebraic rank arguments
technique_cards.output_signature: an exponential upper bound on the size of a subset avoiding the specified additive pattern

input_match.requirement: a finite vector space F_q^n or comparable finite-field product structure
a forbidden additive pattern expressible by a low-degree polynomial or tensor constraint
input_match.evidence: Set.IsAPOfLength gives a 3-term arithmetic progression condition, but not a finite-field tensor constraint

input_match.requirement: a subset whose size is to be bounded by algebraic rank arguments
input_match.evidence: The theorem asks for finiteness of certain 3-term configurations, not a direct upper bound on a large progression-free set

known_obstructions: The powerful numbers form an infinite ordered subsequence of the integers, not a finite vector space over F_q.
The target property concerns consecutive powerful numbers, which adds an order/gap constraint absent from the cap-set setting.
Slice-rank bounds are designed for large subsets avoiding progressions, while the open problem asks for finiteness of special progressions that do occur.

technique_cards.name: hypergraph removal lemma / arithmetic removal lemma

seminal_paper.citation: B. Green, A Szemeredi-type regularity lemma in abelian groups, with applications, Geom. Funct. Anal. 15(2):340-376, 2005.

technique_cards.what_it_does: The arithmetic removal lemma turns a small count of linear configurations in finite abelian groups into a small deletion set that removes all such configurations. For 3-term progressions, its catalog signature concerns solutions of x+z=2y among specified subsets of a finite ambient group.
input_requirements: a finite abelian ambient group G, or a finite interval modeled inside such a group
three subsets X,Y,Z of G with a quantitative upper bound on solutions to x+z=2y
the target configuration is expressible as the linear equation x+z=2y
a removal-style conclusion, allowing deletion of o(|G|) or epsilon|G| elements from the input subsets
technique_cards.output_signature: If the number of 3-term AP solutions is sufficiently small, delete a small number of elements from each input set so that no such solutions remain.

input_match.requirement: a finite abelian ambient group G, or a finite interval modeled inside such a group
input_match.evidence: no match: the target statement is a global finiteness question about a deterministic sequence in ℕ.

input_match.requirement: three subsets X,Y,Z of G with a quantitative upper bound on solutions to x+z=2y
input_match.evidence: no match: the statement provides no density or solution-count hypothesis over finite subsets.

input_match.requirement: the target configuration is expressible as the linear equation x+z=2y
input_match.evidence: Mathlib predicate Set.IsAPOfLength P.toSet 3 appears in the target statement.

input_match.requirement: a removal-style conclusion, allowing deletion of o(|G|) or epsilon|G| elements from the input subsets
input_match.evidence: no match: the requested conclusion is finiteness of consecutive powerful-number triples, not AP elimination by deletion.

known_obstructions: Powerful numbers have zero density, while arithmetic removal lemmas are density tools in finite ambient groups.
The consecutive-term condition is an order constraint on the powerful-number sequence, not part of the linear equation x+z=2y.
A removal conclusion does not directly produce finiteness of exact consecutive triples.

technique_cards.name: Currie-Rampersad Template Method

seminal_paper.citation: James D. Currie and Narad Rampersad, "A proof of Dejean's conjecture," Mathematics of Computation 80(274):1063-1070, 2011.

technique_cards.what_it_does: The method certifies repetition-avoidance properties for infinite words produced by carefully chosen morphisms. It packages global repetition-threshold claims into finite checks involving encodings, kernel repetitions, and bounded forbidden configurations.
input_requirements: an infinite word over a finite alphabet, typically generated by a uniform morphism or equivalent finite generator
the target property expressed as avoidance of fractional powers or finite forbidden templates in that word
a Pansiot-style encoding or comparable finite-state encoding preserving periods and exponents
an algebraic condition on the morphism, such as compatibility with a finite permutation semigroup
a bounded exhaustive check of candidate bad factors or kernel repetitions
technique_cards.output_signature: a certified infinite word avoiding the specified repetitions or templates, usually reducing the global avoidance claim to a finite verification certificate

input_match.requirement: an infinite word over a finite alphabet, typically generated by a uniform morphism or equivalent finite generator
input_match.evidence: Prior slot A3 supplies a gap-sequence word viewpoint, but the gap alphabet is not finite and no morphic generator is identified.

input_match.requirement: the target property expressed as avoidance of fractional powers or finite forbidden templates in that word
input_match.evidence: Prior slot A3 maps a consecutive 3-term AP to an adjacent equal-gap pattern, but only as a local square condition.

input_match.requirement: a Pansiot-style encoding or comparable finite-state encoding preserving periods and exponents
an algebraic condition on the morphism, such as compatibility with a finite permutation semigroup
a bounded exhaustive check of candidate bad factors or kernel repetitions

known_obstructions: Powerful numbers are defined by multiplicative prime-valuation conditions rather than by a morphic fixed point.
The natural gap sequence has unbounded integer symbols, unlike the finite alphabets used in the template method.
The desired conclusion is finiteness of occurrences, not construction of an infinite object avoiding all forbidden repetitions.
No Pansiot encoding, kernel-repetition structure, or finite verification bound is visible from the stated Lean problem.

technique_cards.name: Hypergraph removal lemma

seminal_paper.citation: V. Rodl, B. Nagle, J. Skokan, M. Schacht, and Y. Kohayakawa, The hypergraph regularity method and its applications, Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U.S.A. 102(23):8109-8113, 2005.

technique_cards.what_it_does: The hypergraph removal lemma says that a large finite uniform hypergraph with few copies of a fixed small hypergraph can be made copy-free by deleting few edges. It is a general-purpose counting/deletion principle behind several density results, including multidimensional Szemeredi-type theorems and corner configurations.
input_requirements: a fixed k-uniform hypergraph H whose copies encode the target structured configuration
a finite ambient hypergraph sequence with vertex sets growing to size n
an asymptotic upper bound saying the ambient hypergraphs contain few copies of H
a deletion/removal interpretation connecting removal of hyperedges to elimination of the target number-theoretic configurations
a density or pseudorandomness framework strong enough to model the relevant set inside a grid or product space
technique_cards.output_signature: For fixed H and epsilon, constants delta and n0 such that any n-vertex k-uniform hypergraph with at most delta*n^v(H) copies of H can be made H-free by deleting at most epsilon*n^k edges.

input_match.requirement: a fixed k-uniform hypergraph H whose copies encode the target structured configuration
input_match.evidence: Set.IsAPOfLength identifies the 3-term AP configuration, but no hypergraph encoding is present.

input_match.requirement: a finite ambient hypergraph sequence with vertex sets growing to size n
an asymptotic upper bound saying the ambient hypergraphs contain few copies of H
a deletion/removal interpretation connecting removal of hyperedges to elimination of the target number-theoretic configurations
a density or pseudorandomness framework strong enough to model the relevant set inside a grid or product space

known_obstructions: Powerful numbers form a sparse one-dimensional sequence, while the technique is naturally dense/asymptotic in finite hypergraphs or grids.
The target asks for finiteness of APs among consecutive powerful numbers, not for existence of configurations in a positive-density set.
Consecutiveness in the ordered sequence of powerful numbers has no evident hypergraph-removal deletion analogue.
The supplied statement contains no multidimensional grid model or pseudorandom majorant for powerful numbers.

honest_disclaimer: This dossier catalogs cross-domain analogs and named techniques. It does NOT claim any technique closes the gap. fit_score is a structural overlap heuristic, NOT a probability of success. Stage 4 must still write a fusion sketch that does not propose a bridge lemma.
